1: A direct connection. Josiah keeps the Passover on the fourteenth day of the first month — the precise date of Exodus 12:6 (the original Passover) and Numbers 9:3 (the standardized form). Unlike Hezekiah’s Passover kept in the second month (2 Chronicles 30:2-3, necessitated by Levitical uncleanness), Josiah’s is metronomically exact. The calendar precision is the Chronicler’s signal that the covenant is being kept exactly as given. Christ the Passover Lamb (1 Corinthians 5:7) was crucified on the fourteenth of Nisan (John 19:14,31,42) — the same date Josiah inaugurated. The Passover kept on the day reaches its fulfillment in the sacrifice offered on the day.
2: A shadow. Josiah stationing the priests in their divisions — wayya‘amîdēm ‘al-miškĕmôtām, ‘he caused them to stand upon their stations’ — is the royal-priestly coordination that the Passover requires. The king who ensures the priests are positioned mirrors the pattern of Moses (Exodus 40:12-15) and Solomon (2 Chronicles 8:14). In the new covenant, Christ both positions and is the priest: ‘he always lives to make intercession’ (Hebrews 7:25), the high priest perpetually at his station before the Father. The encouragement Josiah gives the priests (wayhazzeqêm, ‘he strengthened them’) anticipates Christ who equips his priestly people (Ephesians 4:11-12).
3: A shadow. Josiah’s instruction to the Levites — ‘Put the holy ark in the house that Solomon son of David, king of Israel, built. You need not carry it on your shoulders’ — releases the Levites from the transportation burden and redirects their service to worship and preparation. The ark at rest is the typological precedent for Hebrews’ argument: now that the high priest has entered the true sanctuary (Hebrews 9:11-12), the preparatory carrying work is complete. The Levites can now serve the people in preparation rather than transport. Christ, having carried the burden of atonement to its destination, releases his servants for ministry rather than the carrying of sacred burdens.
4: A shadow. ‘Prepare yourselves according to your ancestral families and divisions, as prescribed in the writing of David king of Israel and as written in the document of Solomon his son’ — the coordinated preparation that requires both David’s organizational blueprint and Solomon’s written instructions. The Passover is prepared according to written authority, not improvised. The written authority behind the Passover points to the written word that governs the new covenant Passover: Christ’s command at the Last Supper was immediately transmitted as written apostolic tradition (1 Corinthians 11:23: ‘I received from the Lord what I also delivered to you’). The supper is observed according to writing.
5: A shadow. ‘Stand in the Holy Place according to the groupings of the ancestral families of your brothers the lay people, and according to the Levitical division of a family’ — the ordered positioning of priests between the lay people and the sanctuary is the priestly mediation structure of the Passover. Every lay Israelite approaches through a priest; every priest stands at the boundary of the Holy Place. The mediation structure that positions the priest between the people and the divine presence is the type Christ fulfills: ‘there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus’ (1 Timothy 2:5). The Levitical positioning is the structural type of the singular mediation of Christ.
6: A direct connection. ‘Slaughter the Passover offerings, consecrate yourselves, and prepare your brothers so that they may do according to the word of the LORD by Moses’ — the three-part commission (slaughter, consecrate, prepare) mirrors the three movements of the gospel: sacrifice (Christ’s death), sanctification (the Spirit’s work), and preparation of the church (apostolic teaching). The command to prepare brothers echoes Deuteronomy 16:1-8; the ‘word of the LORD by Moses’ is the authority structure the Passover operates within. Paul applies the same three movements in 1 Corinthians 5:7-8: Christ as sacrifice, the church as the unleavened community, the Passover kept in sincerity and truth.
7: A type. Josiah’s personal contribution — 30,000 lambs and young goats for Passover offerings, plus 3,000 cattle — is the royal gift that makes the Passover possible for the people who cannot provide for themselves. The king provides what the lay people need for the covenant meal. Christ provides what no human could supply for the true Passover: ‘Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed’ (1 Corinthians 5:7). The Passover lamb is not the worshipper’s contribution but the gift of the one who ensures the feast can occur. Josiah’s 30,000 lambs is a vast royal provision that is entirely superseded by the one Lamb who was slain (Revelation 5:12).
8: A shadow. The voluntary contributions of Josiah’s officials — Hilkiah, Zechariah, and Jehiel giving 2,600 lambs and 300 cattle — is the willing generosity of the priestly community that supplements the royal provision. The leaders who give willingly out of their own resources for the community feast mirror the apostolic generosity in Acts 4:34-35 (those with resources selling and distributing) and 2 Corinthians 8:2-3 (the Macedonian churches giving ‘beyond their ability’ for others). The Passover feast that depends on the generosity of leaders pointing toward the community that gathers at Christ’s table because those entrusted with resources have given for the benefit of all.
9: A shadow. The additional Levitical contributions — 5,000 Passover offerings and 500 cattle — represent the Levites as community whose leaders also contribute. The layered generosity (king → officials → Levites) mirrors the NT pattern of giving: from the highest (the apostolic foundation) through the leadership to the whole community. The cumulative total of contributions for one Passover — thousands of animals from multiple community sectors — is the shadow of the one perfect sacrifice that Christ makes once for all (Hebrews 9:26-28: ‘to put away sin by the sacrifice of himself...once for all’), rendering all subsequent animal-sacrifice contributions permanently unnecessary.
10: A shadow. ‘When the service had been prepared, the priests stood in their positions and the Levites in their divisions, according to the king’s command’ — the entire liturgical assembly organized and ready at the king’s command is the Passover as a coordinated act of obedience. The Passover does not happen spontaneously; it is organized, commanded, and executed. The new covenant gathering for the Lord’s Supper follows the same pattern: Paul’s instruction to the Corinthians (1 Corinthians 11:17-34) is explicitly about how it must be ‘prepared’ — the disorder he condemns is a failure of the coordination that Josiah’s Passover models perfectly.
11: A direct connection. ‘They slaughtered the Passover lambs, and the priests sprinkled the blood received from the Levites’ — the blood-reception and blood-sprinkling is the central act of the Passover rite. The Levites slaughter; the priests receive the blood; the priests sprinkle. This three-step mediation of the sacrificial blood is the type of Christ’s atonement: Christ is both the lamb slaughtered and the high priest who presents the blood (Hebrews 9:12: ‘he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption’). In Christ, the slaughterer, the blood-bearer, and the sprinkler are one person — the mediatory chain collapsed into the one Mediator.
12: A shadow. The burnt offerings set aside for distribution ‘to give them to the groupings of the ancestral families of the lay people to offer to the LORD, as written in the Book of Moses’ — the exact distribution of the sacrifice according to written prescription ensures every family group participates. No family is left out; every household has access to the sacrifice through the organized distribution system. In Christ, the distribution of the sacrifice is universal: ‘the grace of God has appeared, bringing salvation for all people’ (Titus 2:11). The Passover distributed to every family group is the type of the gospel offered to every people group (Revelation 5:9: ‘from every tribe and language and people and nation’).
13: A direct connection. ‘They roasted the Passover lambs over the fire as prescribed’ — the roasting method specified in Exodus 12:8-9 (‘roasted over the fire, with its head, legs, and inner parts’; ‘do not eat it raw or boiled in water’) is followed exactly. The bāʾēš (in/by fire) method is the prescribed form. The fire-roasting of the Passover lamb anticipates the cross: the lamb that bore the judgment represented by fire (fire as divine judgment throughout the OT: Sodom, the incense of Nadab and Abihu, the test of Elijah). John 19:28-37 reads the crucifixion through Passover lenses: the unbroken bones (Exodus 12:46; Numbers 9:12) fulfilled (John 19:36). The lamb roasted exactly as prescribed is the type of the death that happened exactly as written (Luke 24:44-46).
14: A shadow. ‘Afterward they prepared for themselves and for the priests, because the priests, the sons of Aaron, were occupied with offering the burnt offerings and the fat portions until night’ — the Levites serve the priests who are too occupied with the sacrificial ministry to prepare their own portions. The servant-of-the-servant principle: the Levites serve the priests so the priests can serve the people so the people can approach YHWH. The inverted service hierarchy (the lesser serves the greater so the greater can serve the even greater who serve the greatest) is the kingdom pattern that Christ names: ‘whoever would be great among you must be your servant, and whoever would be first among you must be slave of all’ (Mark 10:43-44).
15: A shadow. The singers of Asaph in their assigned places ‘according to the command of David, Asaph, Heman, and Jeduthun the king’s seer’ — the Passover is accompanied by prescribed music as the Levitical gatekeepers guard every gate. The Passover feast has a liturgical music tradition; it does not take place in silence. At the Last Supper, Jesus and the disciples ‘sang a hymn’ before going to the Mount of Olives (Matthew 26:30) — almost certainly Pss 114-118, the Hallel, which the Passover seder required. The singers at their stations for Josiah’s Passover and the hymn at the end of the Last Supper are the Passover’s musical frame, pointing to the praise that surrounds the sacrifice of the true Lamb.
16: A shadow. ‘So all the service of the LORD was prepared that day for keeping the Passover and offering burnt offerings on the altar of the LORD, according to the command of King Josiah’ — the completeness formula that signals the Passover has been kept exactly as commanded. The threefold emphasis: all the service, that day, according to command. The precision matters. Paul’s formula for the Lord’s Supper closes with the same completeness concern: ‘as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord’s death until he comes’ (1 Corinthians 11:26). The ‘until he comes’ is the timeline of the new covenant Passover — kept precisely, repeatedly, until the return of the one who instituted it.
17: A shadow. ‘The Israelites who were present kept the Passover at that time, and the Feast of Unleavened Bread for seven days’ — the coupling of Passover with the seven-day Feast of Unleavened Bread is the full liturgical unit (Exodus 12:14-20; 2 Chronicles 13:3-10). The one night of the Passover lamb opens into a week of unleavened bread — the day of sacrifice extends into a season of purification. Paul applies this to the church: ‘let us therefore celebrate the festival, not with the old leaven... but with the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth’ (1 Corinthians 5:8). The seven-day season of the church’s life is to be lived as the continuation of the Passover — the sacrifice applied to daily existence.
18: A direct connection. ‘No Passover like this had been kept in Israel since the days of Samuel the prophet. None of the kings of Israel had kept such a Passover as was kept by Josiah’ — the climactic Passover of the entire Davidic era; nothing like it since the tribal period before the monarchy. This is the peak of Passover observance in the OT — the most complete, most exactly prescribed, most broadly participated feast the covenant people ever kept. It is the final great OT Passover before the exile. The Last Supper is its fulfillment: ‘this is my body... this cup is the new covenant in my blood’ (Luke 22:19-20). The greatest Passover of the old covenant (Josiah’s) gives way to the one feast that renders all future Passover lamb-slaughter unnecessary.
19: A shadow. ‘This Passover was kept in the eighteenth year of Josiah’s reign’ — the dating formula anchors the greatest Passover in the Davidic chronology. The eighteenth year is the year of the Book of the Law’s discovery (2 Chronicles 34:8) — the Passover grows directly out of the rediscovery of the covenant text. The connection between the found law and the Passover celebration is the pattern of covenant renewal: hear the word → repent → keep the feast. Jesus quotes Scripture throughout his final Passover night (John 13-17 is saturated with OT allusion), and the institution of the Lord’s Supper is itself a scriptural interpretation of the Exodus event. The word precedes the feast.
20: A shadow. ‘After all this, when Josiah had put the temple in order, Neco king of Egypt came up to fight at Carchemish on the Euphrates, and Josiah went out to meet him’ — the abrupt transition from the greatest Passover to the king’s last battle is one of the Bible’s most jarring narrative moves. The reforming king who kept the perfect Passover marches to his death. The juxtaposition is the shadow of the Last Supper’s own narrative transition: from the Passover meal (Luke 22:14-23) to Gethsemane (Luke 22:39-46) to the arrest. The greatest feast precedes the greatest death in both cases. The Passover and the cross are inseparable.
21: A revelation of God. Neco’s message to Josiah — ‘I am not coming against you today but against the house with which I am at war. God has commanded me to hurry. Stop opposing God, who is with me, so he will not destroy you’ — is the Egyptian pagan king carrying a genuine divine message that Josiah fails to heed. YHWH’s word comes through an unexpected instrument: the enemy king on his way to Carchemish. The same pattern recurs throughout Scripture: Balaam’s donkey, Cyrus as YHWH’s shepherd (Isaiah 44:28), Nebuchadnezzar as YHWH’s servant (Jeremiah 25:9), and ultimately — the revelation of God speaking through the Gentile instrument that Jesus identifies in Matthew 12:42 (the Queen of the South) and Luke 11:31. The word of God is not confined to Israel’s official prophets.
22: A shadow. ‘Nevertheless Josiah did not turn away from him but disguised himself to fight with him. He did not listen to the words of Neco from the mouth of God’ — Josiah’s fatal mistake: the same disguise strategy as Ahab (2 Chronicles 18:29), and the same failure to receive divine communication through an unexpected channel. The parallels between Josiah and Ahab are deliberate: both disguised themselves for a battle they should not have fought; both died from archers’ arrows in that battle. The righteous king who makes one fatal blunder shadows the human need for a king who will ‘not rely on his own understanding’ (Proverbs 3:5). The one king who never failed to hear the Father — even when the message came through unexpected channels — is Christ (John 5:19: ‘the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing’).
23: A shadow. ‘The archers shot King Josiah, and the king said to his servants, “Take me away, for I am badly wounded”’ — the arrow that struck Josiah mirrors the arrow that killed Ahab (2 Chronicles 18:33-34: a randomly-aimed arrow that found its target). But with a crucial difference: Ahab’s death was YHWH’s judgment on the wicked; Josiah’s death is the suffering of the righteous king who made one fatal error of judgment. The righteous king who suffers mortal wounds in a battle he misjudged is the type of Christ, who — in an entirely different register — voluntarily entered the arena of judgment and bore wounds he did not deserve. The wounding of the king at the moment of his greatest strength is a shadow of the suffering of the innocent.
24: A shadow. Josiah’s servants transferring him from his war chariot to his second chariot and bringing him to Jerusalem, where he died — and the whole of Judah and Jerusalem mourning for him — is the transport of the dying king back to the royal city. The king who cannot save himself, who must be carried by others, who dies in the city of YHWH’s house — this is the shadow of the one who was taken down from the cross and buried in Jerusalem. Joseph of Arimathea’s transport of Jesus’ body (John 19:38-42) is the same movement: from the place of death, carried by servants, to a burial in the holy city. Josiah’s tomb and Jesus’ tomb are both in Jerusalem; one opened permanently on the third day.
25: A direct connection. ‘Jeremiah lamented for Josiah, and all the male and female singers have spoken of Josiah in their laments to this day’ — the prophetic lament for the fallen righteous king becomes a liturgical tradition that endures across generations. The lament for the lost king is one of the OT’s most persistent prophetic forms. Zechariah 12:10-11 explicitly draws on the Megiddo mourning (‘as one mourns for an only son... as one mourns over the firstborn... as the mourning of Hadad-rimmon in the plain of Megiddo’) as the type of the mourning that will occur when Israel looks on ‘him whom they have pierced.’ The lament for Josiah becomes the prophetic template for the lament at the piercing of Christ — the mourning that will flow when the ultimate Shepherd-King is recognized.
26: A shadow. ‘The rest of the acts of Josiah and his faithful deeds, as written in the law of the LORD’ — the standard closing formula, but with a distinctive addition: his ḥasādāyw (‘his acts of faithful covenant loyalty’) as written in the law of the LORD. Josiah’s acts of covenant loyalty are evaluated against the written Torah — his faithfulness is measured by the written word. The standard Christ sets for judgment is himself (John 12:48: ‘the word that I have spoken will judge him on the last day’) and the fulfillment of the written word (Matthew 5:17: ‘do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them’). The written law is the standard; Christ is its fulfillment.
27: A shadow. ‘His acts, first and last, are recorded in the Book of the Kings of Israel and Judah’ — the final closing formula for Josiah; after him come four failed kings in rapid succession (2 Chronicles 36) before the exile. Josiah is the last righteous king; his record is closed. The pattern of the Chronicles is: the righteous king whose work is complete, followed by the collapse. Christ is the one whose works are recorded not in Chronicles but in the Gospels — and John 21:25 says the record is necessarily selective: ‘were every one of them to be written, I suppose that the world itself could not contain the books.’ The chronicler closes with Josiah; the evangelist opens with the one whose acts exceed all record.
